subtopic_detail: Use of vaginal progesterone in threatened miscarriage with previous miscarriage history
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 33-year-old woman at 8 weeks of gestation by last menstrual period with 24 hours of light vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramping. She has had two prior first-trimester miscarriages. There is no shoulder-tip pain, no syncope, and no products passed. Her observations are stable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a viable intrauterine pregnancy with crown rump length matching dates and fetal heart pulsation 152 bpm.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 78, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, speculum shows closed cervix with minimal blood in posterior fornix
data_table.Bloods: Quantitative beta hCG 38000 IU/L, haemoglobin 122 g/L, rhesus negative
data_table.Ultrasound: Single viable intrauterine pregnancy, crown rump length 17 mm, fetal heart rate 152 bpm, no subchorionic haematoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intramuscular medroxyprogesterone 150 mg weekly until 16 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Intramuscular medroxyprogesterone is not used in threatened miscarriage and carries unnecessary risk. (Source: PRISM trial; RCOG green-top 25)

options.text: Commence oral medroxyprogesterone 10 mg twice daily until 12 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Oral progestogens have not been shown to improve outcomes in this setting and are not recommended. (Source: PRISM trial; RCOG green-top 25)

options.text: Reassure and arrange follow-up scan in 2 weeks without progesterone supplementation now
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone modestly improves live birth rates in women with vaginal bleeding plus a history of previous miscarriage, so reassurance alone misses an evidence-based intervention. (Source: PRISM trial; RCOG green-top 25)

options.text: Commence vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg twice daily until 16 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg twice daily until 16 weeks improves live birth rates in women with vaginal bleeding and one or more previous miscarriages, based on the PRISM trial and adopted in RANZCOG and RCOG guidance. (Source: PRISM trial; RCOG green-top 25)

options.text: Refer for inpatient admission and intravenous hydration with serial beta hCG monitoring
options.rationale: Admission is unnecessary for stable threatened miscarriage with a viable scan and beta hCG is not the relevant follow-up once viability is confirmed. (Source: PRISM trial; RCOG green-top 25)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage describes vaginal bleeding before 20 weeks with a closed cervix and a viable intrauterine pregnancy. The 2019 PRISM trial demonstrated a benefit of vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg twice daily in women with bleeding and at least one previous miscarriage, continued until 16 weeks of gestation. The number needed to treat is greatest in women with three or more previous miscarriages. Women without previous miscarriage do not benefit. Anti-D 250 IU IM is given to Rh-negative women with significant first-trimester bleeding after 12 weeks; before 12 weeks the threshold depends on local policy.
key_points: Threatened miscarriage = bleeding plus closed cervix plus viable pregnancy
Vaginal micronised progesterone 400 mg BD until 16 weeks helps subgroup with prior miscarriage
Greatest NNT benefit in women with 3 or more previous miscarriages
No benefit demonstrated in women with no previous miscarriage
Consider anti-D in Rh-negative women per local thresholds
explanation.pearls: Vaginal route is preferred because of higher endometrial bioavailability of micronised progesterone compared with oral administration, where first-pass metabolism is substantial.

subtopic_detail: Counselling on success and risk for VBAC after one prior caesarean for non-recurring indication
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in an antenatal clinic seeing a 34-year-old G2P1 woman at 36 weeks of gestation. Her first baby was born by lower segment caesarean section at term for breech presentation. She is keen to discuss the option of vaginal birth this time. The current pregnancy is uncomplicated, the baby is cephalic with an estimated weight on the 60th centile, and there are no maternal medical comorbidities.

data_table.Obstetric history: First baby 2022, breech at term, elective LSCS, no labour, no infection, baby 3.4 kg
data_table.Current pregnancy: Cephalic, EFW on 60th centile, placenta anterior fundal, normal liquor
data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height 36 cm, BP 116/72 mmHg, dipstick negative
data_table.Imaging: Anatomy and growth scan confirm normal placental position and no scar dehiscence

lead_in: Which counselling statement is most accurate?

options.text: Trial of labour is contraindicated because of the previous caesarean delivery scar status
options.rationale: A single previous LSCS for non-recurring indication is not a contraindication; VBAC is a reasonable choice. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: VBAC success in this scenario is approximately 70 to 75 percent with uterine rupture risk near 0.5 percent
options.rationale: Australian audit and international meta-analyses report VBAC success rates around 70 to 75 percent after non-recurring indication, with uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5 percent. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Induction of labour using prostaglandin pessaries is the safest path to a successful VBAC outcome
options.rationale: Prostaglandin induction substantially increases rupture risk and is generally avoided in scarred uteri. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Continuous electronic fetal monitoring is unnecessary if she is admitted in established spontaneous labour
options.rationale: Continuous CTG monitoring is recommended in labour after caesarean for early detection of fetal heart rate abnormalities heralding scar dehiscence. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

options.text: Epidural analgesia must be declined as it masks the abdominal pain of uterine rupture in labour
options.rationale: Epidural analgesia is not contraindicated; pain breakthrough or fetal heart abnormalities remain reliable rupture signals. (Source: RANZCOG: Birth after previous caesarean)

explanation.summary: Vaginal birth after caesarean is a reasonable option for most women after one previous low transverse caesarean for non-recurring indication. Success rates are approximately 70 to 75 percent in pooled cohorts. Uterine rupture risk is approximately 0.5 percent overall in spontaneous labour, rising with induction (especially with prostaglandins), augmentation with oxytocin, short inter-delivery interval (under 18 months), and macrosomia. Continuous electronic fetal monitoring is recommended because fetal heart rate decelerations are the earliest sign of dehiscence. Decisions should be supported with written information and documented preference.
key_points: VBAC success around 70 to 75 percent after non-recurring indication
Uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5 percent in spontaneous labour
Prostaglandin induction substantially increases rupture risk
Continuous CTG in labour after caesarean is recommended
Epidural does not contraindicate VBAC and does not mask rupture
explanation.pearls: Mechanical induction using a Foley catheter has a more favourable safety profile in women planning VBAC than prostaglandins when induction is indicated.

subtopic_detail: Antenatal planning for suspected placenta accreta spectrum disorder
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 36-year-old G4P3 woman at 28 weeks of gestation. She has had three previous caesarean sections. The mid-trimester anatomy scan reported an anterior placenta extending across the previous scar. A targeted 24-week ultrasound described loss of the retroplacental clear zone, multiple placental lacunae, and increased subplacental vascularity. She has had no bleeding and is otherwise well in the current pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 84, BP 116/72 mmHg, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Imaging summary: Anterior placenta praevia covering os, loss of retroplacental clear zone, lacunae, bridging vessels, possible bladder wall invasion
data_table.Examination: Uterus appropriate size for dates, no PV bleeding, no contractions
data_table.Bloods: Haemoglobin 118 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, ferritin 36 micrograms/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Plan vaginal delivery after 39 weeks with a cell-salvage trolley available in theatre
options.rationale: Placenta praevia with accreta spectrum is an absolute contraindication to vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta accreta spectrum)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound at 36 weeks and plan caesarean section after spontaneous labour onset
options.rationale: Planned caesarean hysterectomy is scheduled before spontaneous labour, not after, to avoid catastrophic bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta accreta spectrum)

options.text: Refer to a tertiary placenta accreta multidisciplinary centre for planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks
options.rationale: Suspected placenta accreta spectrum should be managed at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team and planned caesarean hysterectomy typically at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids, with cell salvage and interventional radiology available. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta accreta spectrum)

options.text: Arrange elective uterine artery embolisation now and review imaging again at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Prophylactic embolisation before delivery is not standard; interventional radiology is used intra or post-operatively in selected cases. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta accreta spectrum)

options.text: Discharge to a remote community hospital and plan delivery there with on-call surgical staff
options.rationale: Delivery at a non-tertiary site lacking interventional radiology, blood bank, and gynaecological oncology back-up substantially increases maternal mortality. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta accreta spectrum)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) risk rises sharply with each prior caesarean delivery especially in the presence of placenta praevia (approximately 40 percent risk after three prior caesareans with anterior praevia). Ultrasound features include loss of the retroplacental clear zone, placental lacunae, abnormal uteroplacental interface, and bridging vessels into bladder. MRI is reserved for posterior placentas or assessment of parametrial extension. Definitive management is planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks at a tertiary centre after corticosteroid course, with multidisciplinary team, cell salvage, blood bank, and interventional radiology on standby. Conservative uterus-preserving approaches are reserved for selected cases at expert centres.
key_points: Three prior caesareans plus anterior praevia carry approximately 40 percent PAS risk
Ultrasound features: lost clear zone, lacunae, bridging vessels
Plan caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids
Multidisciplinary tertiary centre with cell salvage and IR
Vaginal birth and elective embolisation are not standard
explanation.pearls: If antenatal imaging is equivocal but suspicion is high, formal MRI with PAS protocol can refine surgical planning especially when posterior placenta limits ultrasound assessment.

subtopic_detail: Workup of cyclical dyschezia and rectovaginal nodule suggesting deep infiltrating endometriosis
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 31-year-old nulliparous woman with a 3-year history of cyclical pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and worsening cyclical dyschezia and rectal bleeding only during menstruation. She has tried two combined oral contraceptive pills with breakthrough pain. A standard transabdominal ultrasound 6 months ago was reported as normal. She is keen to preserve fertility and wants to know what to do next.

data_table.Examination: Tender posterior fornix with a palpable nodule, fixed retroverted uterus, no adnexal mass
data_table.Bowel symptoms: Cyclical rectal bleeding only with periods, no melaena, no weight loss
data_table.Bloods: Haemoglobin 118 g/L, ferritin 22 micrograms/L, CA-125 64 U/mL
data_table.Sexual history: Single partner, contraception nil currently, last STI screen normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Refer urgently for colonoscopy to exclude colorectal malignancy before any gynaecological workup
options.rationale: Cyclical rectal bleeding strictly with menses points to bowel endometriosis rather than malignancy; colonoscopy adds little here and is not first line. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Commence empirical GnRH agonist therapy with norethisterone add-back without further imaging
options.rationale: Empirical GnRH agonist without mapping deep disease misses surgical planning opportunities and is not first line at this stage. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Order a Mirena intrauterine device and review symptoms over the next 6 months in clinic
options.rationale: Mirena is an effective hormonal option but it does not establish the extent of deep disease in a patient with rectovaginal nodule and bowel symptoms. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Request CA-125 and full blood count and review the response to a 3-month progestin trial
options.rationale: CA-125 lacks specificity and a further drug trial without imaging delays a clearer diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Refer for a specialised transvaginal ultrasound mapping for deep infiltrating endometriosis with bowel protocol
options.rationale: Specialised transvaginal ultrasound by an experienced operator using the deep infiltrating endometriosis bowel protocol is the recommended first imaging in suspected DIE, with MRI reserved for surgical planning when ultrasound is inconclusive. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

explanation.summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is defined by lesions extending more than 5 mm under the peritoneum. The most predictive symptoms are cyclical bowel or bladder pain. The International Deep Endometriosis Analysis (IDEA) group has standardised transvaginal ultrasound assessment for the pouch of Douglas, uterosacral ligaments, vagina, rectum and bladder. Specialist TVUS in experienced hands is the preferred first imaging and can identify rectovaginal nodules. Pelvic MRI is reserved for ultrasound non-diagnostic or surgical planning. Treatment combines hormonal suppression with multidisciplinary surgical planning (gynaecology and colorectal) in advanced disease.
key_points: Cyclical dyschezia and rectal bleeding suggest deep bowel endometriosis
Standard transabdominal ultrasound often misses DIE
Specialised TVUS with IDEA protocol is first-line imaging
MRI is reserved for surgical planning when TVUS inconclusive
Combine hormonal therapy with multidisciplinary surgical planning
explanation.pearls: The sliding sign test on transvaginal ultrasound assesses pouch of Douglas obliteration; absent sliding has high specificity for rectovaginal endometriosis.

subtopic_detail: Workup and management of recurrent miscarriage with positive antiphospholipid antibodies
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in a recurrent miscarriage clinic reviewing a 34-year-old woman who has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages. She is otherwise well, has had no thromboembolic events, and her last pregnancy was lost at 9 weeks. She had karyotype testing on the last products of conception which was 46,XX. Her partner has a child from a previous relationship. Initial screening bloods are back.

data_table.Initial screen: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, normal renal and liver function, fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L
data_table.Thrombophilia: Lupus anticoagulant positive on 2 occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG 60 GPL units, beta-2 glycoprotein 1 IgG 55 units
data_table.Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound normal cavity, no fibroid or polyp

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan to commence in her next pregnancy?

options.text: Low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily commencing preconception plus prophylactic enoxaparin from positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome with three or more consecutive early miscarriages is treated with low-dose aspirin from preconception plus prophylactic LMWH from positive pregnancy test until 6 weeks postpartum. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Warfarin INR target 2.0 to 3.0 from preconception until delivery to maintain anticoagulation
options.rationale: Warfarin is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy; LMWH is the chosen anticoagulant. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 10 mg daily from preconception until 12 weeks of gestation to suppress immune response
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy does not improve outcomes in obstetric APS and is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin infusion at 6, 10 and 14 weeks of gestation as primary therapy
options.rationale: IVIG is not first-line in obstetric APS and trial evidence does not support routine use. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy 400 mg daily without aspirin or anticoagulation considered
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is adjunctive in some APS protocols but does not replace aspirin and LMWH as the established regimen. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid syndrome is the most reliably treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage. Sapporo criteria require persistent positive lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin or anti-beta-2 glycoprotein-1 on at least two occasions 12 weeks apart plus clinical criteria (3 or more consecutive early losses, one or more late losses at 10 weeks or beyond, or severe placental insufficiency). Treatment of obstetric APS is low-dose aspirin commencing preconception plus prophylactic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg daily) from positive pregnancy test until 6 weeks postpartum. Karyotype testing of products is recommended after recurrent loss; balanced translocations in one parent require genetic counselling.
key_points: Persistent antibody positivity over 12 weeks plus clinical criteria define APS
Aspirin from preconception plus LMWH from positive pregnancy test
Continue LMWH until 6 weeks postpartum
Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy
Karyotype products and parents to exclude balanced translocations
explanation.pearls: Women with previous arterial thrombosis on a background of APS require therapeutic-dose LMWH rather than prophylactic doses during pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Endometrial sampling threshold in heavy menstrual bleeding with failed levonorgestrel IUS
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 47-year-old G2P2 woman with heavy menstrual bleeding refractory to a levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 9 months ago. She has had no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. Her BMI is 36, she has type 2 diabetes on metformin, and she has no personal history of breast cancer. Pelvic ultrasound after IUS insertion was reported as normal endometrium with the device correctly positioned.

data_table.Examination: Threads visible at the cervix, no uterine tenderness, no adnexal masses, BMI 36
data_table.Cycle pattern: Heavy bleeding for 8 days every 23 days, soaking through pads hourly day 2 to 3
data_table.Bloods: Haemoglobin 95 g/L, ferritin 8 micrograms/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L
data_table.Imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound 4 months ago: endometrial thickness 7 mm, no focal lesion, IUS in situ

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Remove the Mirena and trial tranexamic acid plus mefenamic acid during the next two menstrual cycles
options.rationale: Medical options without endometrial sampling miss a potential endometrial pathology in a high-risk woman with failed first-line treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Refer to gynaecology for direct endometrial ablation as the next definitive treatment step
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is contraindicated until endometrial pathology has been excluded by sampling in a woman at high risk of hyperplasia. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Refer for total laparoscopic hysterectomy as the definitive treatment given failed IUS trial
options.rationale: Definitive surgery without histological clarity risks operating with unrecognised malignancy and may alter the surgical approach (subtotal vs total, oncological clearance). (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Refer for hysteroscopy with directed endometrial biopsy to exclude endometrial hyperplasia or cancer
options.rationale: Persistent heavy menstrual bleeding despite a levonorgestrel IUS in a woman with obesity, type 2 diabetes, and aged over 45 meets thresholds for endometrial sampling to exclude hyperplasia or carcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Order a repeat pelvic ultrasound looking for a missed submucous fibroid as the cause of failure
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound was recently done with the device in situ; repeating without sampling is less informative than hysteroscopy with biopsy. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding management starts with exclusion of structural and systemic causes (FBC, ferritin, TSH, pelvic ultrasound), then a hierarchy of medical treatments (levonorgestrel intrauterine system, tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, combined hormonal therapy) before surgical options. Endometrial sampling is indicated in women aged 45 or older, or younger with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia (obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome, tamoxifen, family history of Lynch syndrome, unopposed oestrogen), or with persistent symptoms despite treatment. Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy gives the highest sensitivity for focal lesions compared with blind Pipelle sampling.
key_points: Sample endometrium in women aged 45 plus with persistent bleeding
Sample younger women with hyperplasia risk factors
Hysteroscopy with biopsy outperforms blind sampling for focal lesions
Exclude pathology before ablation or hysterectomy
Treat iron deficiency in parallel
explanation.pearls: Endometrial thickness cut-offs from postmenopausal women do not apply in premenopausal heavy menstrual bleeding; cyclical changes mean any value can be normal at different cycle phases.

subtopic_detail: Valproate pregnancy prevention program in a woman with bipolar I disorder of childbearing potential
stem: You are the consultant psychiatrist reviewing a 28-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder maintained on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 4 years following a manic episode. She has had no further admissions and is now in a stable relationship. She is using condoms only as contraception. She raises the possibility of starting a family within the next 2 years. Liver function and full blood count are normal.

data_table.Bloods: Valproate level 350 micromol/L (therapeutic), LFTs normal, FBC normal, beta hCG negative
data_table.Reproductive history: Nulliparous, regular cycles, no fertility concerns
data_table.Current medications: Sodium valproate 1500 mg daily, no other psychotropics

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose and review again in 6 months when pregnancy planning becomes active
options.rationale: Valproate carries dose-dependent risk of major congenital malformation up to 11 percent and neurodevelopmental disability up to 30 to 40 percent; delaying change is unsafe in a woman of childbearing potential. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Counsel on teratogenicity, switch to lithium, and enrol in the valproate pregnancy prevention program
options.rationale: Australian guidance requires women on valproate to be counselled annually, use highly effective contraception until cessation, and be transitioned off where possible; lithium is the standard maintenance alternative in bipolar I. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Add high-dose folate 5 mg daily and continue valproate at the current dose throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Folate does not negate the high baseline malformation and neurodevelopmental risks of valproate exposure; substitution is required. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 750 mg daily and continue indefinitely as a safer dose in pregnancy
options.rationale: There is no safe valproate dose in pregnancy; risk is dose-related but persists at any therapeutic dose. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program)

options.text: Cease valproate today without alternative and arrange community mental health follow-up review weekly
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation without a replacement mood stabiliser substantially increases relapse risk in a woman with established bipolar I disorder. (Source: RANZCP: Bipolar disorder clinical practice guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP: Bipolar disorder clinical practice guideline

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic burden of any common psychotropic, with major congenital malformation in 9 to 11 percent and dose-related neurodevelopmental disability in 30 to 40 percent of children exposed in utero. The TGA imposed a Pregnancy Prevention Program in 2023 requiring annual specialist review, signed acknowledgement of risk, and use of at least one highly effective contraceptive method (long-acting reversible contraception preferred) in any woman of childbearing potential. Switching to lithium, lamotrigine (better in bipolar II), or olanzapine for maintenance is preferred well before conception, with relapse risk weighed against teratogenicity.
key_points: Major malformation risk 9 to 11 percent on valproate in pregnancy
Neurodevelopmental disability risk approximately 30 to 40 percent
Annual specialist review and signed risk acknowledgement required
Highly effective contraception required while on valproate
Lithium is the standard alternative for bipolar I maintenance
explanation.pearls: If valproate cannot be substituted because of refractory illness, the woman must use a long-acting reversible contraceptive (intrauterine device or implant) and document the decision in the Pregnancy Prevention Program annual review.

sources.label: RANZCP: Bipolar disorder clinical practice guideline

subtopic_detail: Anticholinergic burden when escalating tricyclic antidepressant in an older adult with comorbidities
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 76-year-old man with chronic neuropathic pain and depression maintained on amitriptyline 25 mg nocte for 3 months. He has had a partial mood response and his pain remains poorly controlled. He also takes oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily for urge incontinence, donepezil 10 mg daily for mild Alzheimer dementia, and citalopram 20 mg daily was ceased 2 months ago when amitriptyline was started.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 88, BP 132/80 mmHg supine, 110/72 mmHg standing
data_table.Cognitive status: MMSE 24 of 30, mild short-term recall impairment, attention intact
data_table.Bloods: Sodium 138 mmol/L, eGFR 62 mL/min/1.73m^2, ECG QTc 440 ms
data_table.Medications: Amitriptyline 25 mg nocte, oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily, donepezil 10 mg daily, ramipril 5 mg daily

lead_in: Which medication change is most appropriate?

options.text: Increase amitriptyline to 50 mg nocte for stronger antidepressant and neuropathic effect
options.rationale: Higher anticholinergic burden in dementia worsens cognition and falls and counteracts donepezil; nortriptyline at a low dose is preferred when a tricyclic is needed in older adults. (Source: AMH; eTG)

options.text: Switch amitriptyline to duloxetine 30 mg daily and titrate based on response and tolerability
options.rationale: Duloxetine is a reasonable option but does not address the immediate concurrent anticholinergic burden of oxybutynin and amitriptyline opposing donepezil. (Source: AMH; eTG)

options.text: Increase donepezil to 23 mg daily to overcome the anticholinergic burden affecting cognition
options.rationale: The 23 mg dose is not available in Australia and treating prescriber-introduced anticholinergic burden by escalating a cholinesterase inhibitor is not appropriate. (Source: AMH; eTG)

options.text: Cease oxybutynin and review bladder symptoms with non-anticholinergic alternatives in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Stopping oxybutynin alone is reasonable but does not address the amitriptyline contribution to anticholinergic burden, the dominant culprit. (Source: AMH; eTG)

options.text: Substitute amitriptyline with nortriptyline 10 to 25 mg nocte and cease oxybutynin with bladder retraining
options.rationale: Nortriptyline has substantially lower anticholinergic and orthostatic burden than amitriptyline in older adults and ceasing oxybutynin addresses the other major contributor while donepezil continues. (Source: AMH; eTG)

explanation.summary: Anticholinergic burden in older adults causes cognitive decline, falls, urinary retention, dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, and orthostatic hypotension. Drugs with high burden include tricyclics, especially tertiary amines (amitriptyline, imipramine), oxybutynin, hyoscine, and first-generation antihistamines. Nortriptyline and desipramine are secondary amines with lower anticholinergic burden. In patients on cholinesterase inhibitors for dementia, anticholinergic drugs directly oppose treatment and worsen cognitive trajectory. Bladder retraining, mirabegron, or topical oestrogen are alternatives to oxybutynin for urge incontinence.
key_points: Anticholinergic burden causes falls, cognitive decline and retention
Amitriptyline carries higher burden than nortriptyline
Anticholinergics oppose donepezil effect in dementia
Mirabegron and topical oestrogen are non-anticholinergic incontinence alternatives
Postural hypotension is a marker of cumulative burden
explanation.pearls: The Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden scale gives oxybutynin and amitriptyline 3 points each; combined exposure above 3 points carries clinically significant cognitive risk in older adults.

subtopic_detail: Lorazepam challenge in suspected catatonia complicating major depressive episode
stem: You are the consultant liaison psychiatrist asked to review a 22-year-old man with a known history of severe major depression admitted under medicine for 4 days of not eating or drinking, mutism, posturing, and waxy flexibility. He is medically cleared, with a normal CT brain, lumbar puncture, and metabolic screen. He has had no fevers, autonomic instability, or rigidity. He is not on any antipsychotic.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 88, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Mental state: Mute, immobile, waxy flexibility on right upper limb, no rigidity, no autonomic instability
data_table.Investigations: CK 230 U/L (normal), TSH normal, urine drug screen negative, CT brain and CSF normal
data_table.Bush-Francis catatonia rating: Score 16 with 6 of 14 screening items positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral olanzapine 10 mg daily and review the response again in 48 hours
options.rationale: Antipsychotics may worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are not first-line. (Source: RANZCP catatonia guidance)

options.text: Refer for urgent electroconvulsive therapy without a benzodiazepine trial in the next 24 hours
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective for refractory catatonia but is reserved for failure of the benzodiazepine challenge or malignant catatonia. (Source: RANZCP catatonia guidance)

options.text: Administer parenteral lorazepam 1 to 2 mg and observe over 5 to 10 minutes for clinical response
options.rationale: A parenteral lorazepam challenge produces a marked reduction in catatonic signs within minutes in over half of cases and confirms the diagnosis and pathway. (Source: RANZCP catatonia guidance)

options.text: Start scheduled diazepam 5 mg orally three times a day and review response again in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral diazepam is too slow and pharmacokinetically poor in this context; parenteral lorazepam is the standard test dose and treatment. (Source: RANZCP catatonia guidance)

options.text: Reassure the medical team this is psychogenic and arrange psychotherapy review in the community
options.rationale: Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome with substantial mortality if untreated; dismissing it as psychogenic is unsafe and incorrect. (Source: RANZCP catatonia guidance)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is identified clinically using the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale and confirmed pharmacologically by response to a parenteral lorazepam challenge. The standard dose is 1 to 2 mg IM or IV with marked reduction in catatonic signs within 5 to 10 minutes considered a positive response. Treatment continues with scheduled lorazepam, titrated as tolerated (often 6 to 24 mg daily total in divided doses). Refractory catatonia, malignant catatonia (with autonomic instability and hyperthermia), or NMS-like presentation responds best to ECT. Antipsychotics may exacerbate catatonia and risk NMS, so are avoided unless the catatonia is clearly settled.
key_points: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale screens 14 items
Parenteral lorazepam 1 to 2 mg is diagnostic and therapeutic
Marked reduction in 5 to 10 minutes confirms catatonia
ECT is first-line for malignant or refractory catatonia
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia clearly settled
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (with hyperthermia, autonomic instability, raised CK) is clinically indistinguishable from neuroleptic malignant syndrome and is treated identically with cessation of dopamine antagonists, supportive care, lorazepam, and early ECT.

subtopic_detail: DSM-5 timing rule discriminating schizoaffective disorder from bipolar disorder with psychotic features
stem: You are the consultant psychiatrist reviewing a 27-year-old man on an extended inpatient admission. Over the past 12 months he has had two confirmed episodes of mania each lasting around 2 weeks accompanied by persistent grandiose delusions. Between these manic episodes, the family describe approximately 6 weeks of continued grandiose delusions and auditory hallucinations without mood disturbance. He has had ongoing psychotic symptoms for most of the last 12 months. He is currently euthymic but continues to describe special abilities.

data_table.Substance use: Nil illicit substances on urine drug screen, no alcohol misuse
data_table.Medical workup: CT brain, full medical workup, autoimmune encephalitis screen negative
data_table.Mental state: Euthymic mood, hyperreligious content, paranoid persecutory delusions, no formal thought disorder
data_table.Family history: Father with bipolar I disorder, paternal aunt with schizophrenia

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits the DSM-5 timing rule?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type is the best fit given the longitudinal pattern of symptoms
options.rationale: DSM-5 requires delusions or hallucinations for 2 weeks or more in the absence of a major mood episode, with mood symptoms present for the majority of the total illness duration; both criteria are met here. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features is the most accurate single diagnosis
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychosis only during mood episodes; psychosis here persists outside manic episodes. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid bipolar I disorder is the most accurate combined diagnosis
options.rationale: Comorbid diagnosis is not used when criteria for schizoaffective disorder are met; schizoaffective is the integrating diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with marked stressor is the most accurate diagnosis presently
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms to last less than 1 month; 12 months of psychotic illness excludes this. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder grandiose type is the most accurate single diagnosis in this presentation
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires absence of other psychotic features beyond delusion and minimal functional impairment; hallucinations and manic episodes exclude this. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is the integrating DSM-5 diagnosis when psychosis persists for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode and mood episodes are present for the majority of the total illness. The bipolar subtype includes manic episodes; the depressive subtype includes depressive episodes only. Discrimination from bipolar I with psychotic features hinges on whether psychotic symptoms persist outside mood episodes. Treatment uses second-generation antipsychotics (paliperidone has the only FDA indication) and a mood stabiliser; the choice of lithium or anticonvulsant depends on episode polarity and individual factors.
key_points: Psychosis for 2 weeks or more without mood episode is required
Mood episodes must be present for majority of total illness
Bipolar subtype includes mania; depressive subtype does not
Comorbid schizophrenia plus bipolar is not assigned when criteria met
Antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser is the standard treatment
explanation.pearls: Family history of both schizophrenia and bipolar disorder is over-represented in schizoaffective disorder, supporting it as a distinct genetic entity rather than a coincidence of the two main psychoses.
